title: `podman run --mount` creates empty files on host filesystem
body:
### Issue Description

(Fairly) Minimal reproducer:

```bash
#! /bin/sh

[ -d /tmp/24309 ] && rm -r /tmp/24309

mkdir -p /tmp/24309/dir
echo 'xxx' > /tmp/24309/dir/content # Not significant?
echo 'yyy' > /tmp/24309/dir.arm64

[ -e /tmp/24309/dir/arm64 ] && exit 1
date

podman container run \
  --rm \
  --entrypoint /bin/sh \
  --interactive \
  --tty \
  --mount \
    type=bind,source=/tmp/24309/dir.arm64,destination=/dir/arm64,ro=true \
  --mount \
    type=bind,source=/tmp/24309/dir,destination=/dir,ro=true \
  docker.io/library/ubuntu:24.04 -c exit 0 || exit 2

ls -l /tmp/24309/dir/arm64
[ -e /tmp/24309/dir/arm64 ] && exit 3
```

Output (having copied the above to `test.sh` and made it executable):

```bash
$ sudo ./test.sh ; echo $?
Thu Oct 17 20:56:07 UTC 2024
-rwx------ 1 root root 0 Oct 17 20:56 /tmp/24309/dir/arm64
3
```

In this instance, a root-owned zero-sized file is created on the *host* filesystem in the location where within the container a host file is bind-mounted.

This may be a known-issue or intentional, but it seems unexpected?

(N.B. Removing the empty file `podman` creates from the host filesystem removes the copy of the file from the container, although the original file on the host is untouched)

### Steps to reproduce the issue

See reproducer script above.

### Describe the results you received

A file `/tmp/24309/dir/arm64` is created on the host filesystem, and persists after the container and `podman` process have exited.

### Describe the results you expected

I wouldn't expect even empty files to "leak" from the container environment back to the host.

### podman info output

### Podman in a container

No

### Privileged Or Rootless

Privileged

### Upstream Latest Release

Yes

### Additional environment details

Latter test-case was run on AWS R8g.

### Additional information

This has been happening since around `podman` v2.x, but I've only just figured out a reliable reproducer!

comments.body:
This is expected and not a bug.

Podman will sort the mount order properly for you, so for first mount /dir then /dir/arm64. If we do not do that the file would just be overmounted and not be visible at all.

So now you end up with an empty `/dir` or with other contents such as `content` and you requested to mount to /dir/arm64. It is a requirement by the kernel that the target must exists when doing a mount so the oci runtime will create an empty file there so that in can mount it. Now because `/dir` itself is already a bind mount it will leak onto the host directory, there is no way to avoid this.

… @Luap99, could any file created for this means not at least be removed when the container exits?

And does the name have to be the same - could it not be have a full-stop prepended so that it is hidden by default?

It really cannot, there is no tracking whatsoever. The mount code just creates the files as it does the mounts it is impossible to remove them unless the oci runtimes tracks it and then does that but that likely requires changes for all oci runtimes and might even break other things who knows.

There is no way to hide the file. A bind mount requires the target to exists if you want to rename it you must rename the mount point.
